speaker: interviewer
text: November, November 1, 1995. We're with Lucie Porges in New York City. And we're one tape number five.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of my parents' wedding in Vienna in 1926 in a place called the Seitenstetten-Tempel, which is right next the Jewish community, the Kultusgemeinde, even now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how old they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: They must've been in their late 20s, I would say. This picture was taken just before we left Vienna. It was the same day when we took all our passport photos. My parents decided to do one picture together, and-- because we had very, very few pictures. But that's one that's very precious to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been January, 1939, just before we left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if you could name the people in the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us where you are.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there's my sister, Elfi, to start with, then my mother, me, and my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were how old here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about 11, 11 years old I think. This is a picture from Austria. It was a place somewhere near Vienna where we spent the summer. My mother is the first one on the left, next to her mother, my grandmother. And she's expecting my sister. And I am one of two little kids sitting in front, the third one from the left. And I had very short hair, just like I do now. And I have lots of cousins on it. And lots of them perished. Only two of them went to Israel and are still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the names of others?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the one's there are Erica and Ruth. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are they? Where are they in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: My cousins, next to me, next to me on the left-- and the others perished. One is Bubi. And one is Paula in, in Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the names of those on the-- in the middle row?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just know one of them, Elsa, who is next to the woman who's holding the baby. And the other two girls, I think they are the nurses that took care of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the woman holding the baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: The woman holding the baby is, I think, one of the cousins of my mother, somebody I didn't know too well. I don't remember too well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was, like, three years old, so it must've been in 1929, 1929. My sister was born in 1930, so around that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know the name of the actual location?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just know it was very close to Vienna, a little village. But I don't remember the location. This is my mother's mother, Miriam Rosner. And I adored her. She's the lady who told me all those wonderful stories. And she actually left Vienna-- two of her sons were in Israel-- and died in Israel a few years later. And she's buried at the Mount of Olives. I have been a couple of times to visit her grave. But I never saw her again after Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: When, when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture? That must have been taken in, I think, the 1920s, I would say, this particular picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, again, she left Vienna in what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: She left Vienna in 1938. Her two sons sent her a visa for Israel. And she went there as soon as possible. I mean, she left Vienna to live there. But she was not happy there. It was very difficult for her. She also was very close to my mother. And to be separated from her was very hard for her.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my mother as a young woman. As you can see, she was always very elegant. And this might be where I got the idea of doing fashions and my whole career, actually. Because that's what I did in America for 43 years. I worked for Pauline Trigere, which was one of the best couture houses. And somehow, it must've started there. She's here with her favorite brother and his wife. And we went together to Switzerland over the border with his uncle and aunt. So it all means a lot to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which one is your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother is the one on the left with the big hat and the fur. She was quite beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the experiences of her brother and sister-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, her brother was a very well-to-do store owner in Germany. And he emigrated from Vienna to Paris. He actually stayed in Europe. He managed to stay in Switzerland after we left and went back to Lyon. He managed to stay there. And he even-- I'm not sure if he became Swiss-- but for the rest of his life, he lived in Zurich. I don't know how he managed it, but he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rosner-- his name was Herman Rosner. And her name was Rozhi Rosner. And my mother, after my father died-- actually, he died in Switzerland-- she, my mother, went to Switzerland every year, every summer, to spend time with her brother and sister-in-law. And my father is actually buried in Zurich.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: He died in 1962.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: The date of the picture must be 1925, something like that, this picture. This was a store that my mother's family had in Vienna. She had five brothers. And they just managed very well. They had a chocolate store. And they also had a grocery store. And Mother helped out in either one of them. And my grandmother just didn't work. I mean, she was like a queen. All her sons took care of her. It was very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the address?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was [? Laxen ?] [? Buchengasse. ?] That's what I remember, in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Within the Jewish district?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it wasn't a Jewish district at all. No. The Jewish district in Vienna was the 2nd District, where lots of Jews lived next to each other. And there were Jewish restaurants, et cetera. But this was not. It was a very integrated neighborhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably 1924, 1925, around there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they had the store for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: For many years, I would see well into the '30s. This is my mother with one of her brothers in the grocery store where she worked. And actually, this uncle, by the name of Uncle Bel, emigrated to Israel and had ice cream salons in Tel Aviv for many years until he died. I visited him there, and him and my cousins. And they are still there, of course. They live in Israel. I have two, three, four-- I have six cousins in Israel, still alive, whom I visit every once in awhile. But this uncle died. Most of my uncles died, I mean. They would be 90 by now, over 90.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a different story than the candy shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it's a different store. It's a different store. They had several.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were involved with this after the candy or at the same time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the same time, at the same time-- well, there were five of them, five brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: The date-- I would say any time between 1921 and 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know how long they had the store?

speaker: interviewee
text: For a few years, a few years, a few years-- I don't know how long. I don't really remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the address?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my mother wasn't married then. And this one was [? Schultergasse, ?] I think. This is the only picture I have as a little girl in Vienna. I remember having this tricycle. And when I was photographed, all the other children looked around. And it was quite wonderful to have a tricycle at that time. And that's about it. That's all I remember, having a wonderful thing, a wonderful toy. It was a park where we used to go every day, every afternoon, with my mother and my grandmother. And I think I had a pretty good time. [LAUGHS] That's all I remember. It was long before Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know who took the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I have no idea. I don't remember. But that it-- we could keep this picture for all these years, going through all these different changes is absolutely amazing to me to still have it. But I think it's great. I mean, compare-- I have one picture. I must have hundreds of pictures of my grandchildren. And here, I have one of myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and what was the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I must've been, like, five, so it was probably 1931, something like that. This is one of my favorite pictures of my father. It must've been in 1946 or 1947. My parents had gone back to living in Lyon. And I was still in Switzerland, finishing my studies at the Ecole des Beaux-Arts. And Papa was just visiting me for a couple of days. And we walked on that wonderful cay near the lake in Switzerland. And I really enjoyed being with him alone and talking to him. It was great. He had a wonderful sense of humor. And we enjoyed being together, just the two of us. It was great. It was a wonderful time. I'll never forget it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably 1947 sometimes-- looks like it's spring. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in the winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my-- the passport they gave us in Vienna. We were not Austrians, because my father was not naturalized when he came from Poland. So they gave us a fremdenpass [? pour ?] [? etrangers, ?] which means somebody who doesn't belong, a stranger. And this was the passport-- our travel document actually. We didn't really need it because we didn't have any visas to go with. The way we left over the borders, we didn't need the passport. But I have it any way. And so I kept it all these years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you translate the, the left page?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It says staatenlos, without country, without profession because I was a child. Then it says Vienna, born on the 23rd of November, 1926, Vienna. Stature-- small, face-- oval, eyes-- gray-blue, and hair-- brown.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anywhere, does it-- does it label you as Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think at that time they did it yet. I-- later on, they put a big J, a few months later. I don't think they did it for that passport. But I must look in the front and see.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this issued?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was issued most likely in January, 1939, or in December, 1938, around that time. There must be a date somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: And issued by the Austrians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. It's just like a travel document. But you see the German eagle on it. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] The Germans actually issued that passport because they occupied Austria. And they say on it that I do not have the German nationality. So it's just for etranger, for strangers. And it was done in January, 1940. And actually, it, didn't really help. Because we always went over borders without passports. We had no visas. It was just a document. But not really-- we couldn't use it for traveling, because we had no visas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you issued the document because you were, you were wanting to leave the country, or was everybody--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, right. No, everybody got a document to-- they wanted us to leave the country. But we just couldn't get enough visas, all the people in Vienna. We tried all kinds of countries, but they were not forthco-- forecoming-- forthcoming-- how do you say that? So we just had to find another way to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait? OK. This is the first French document I had, the carte d'identite in Lyon, where I started working, painting scarves. And that was in 1941. Issued by?

speaker: interviewee
text: Issued by the French government in Lyon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what does it say on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: What does it say? [FRENCH SPEECH] See, I never read that thing. For all these years, I never even looked at it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well, that I had the right to do what I was doing, like painting scarves-- that's what it says.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was issued as soon as you entered France the first time?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was before we went to Switzerland. That's when I worked in Lyon. I haven't looked at this thing for so many years. I should have read it before. Anyway, that was the one in 1941. And then in 1942, we went to Switzerland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the carte d'identite I had in Paris when I went there after school in Switzerland. And I was trying to get a card to be able to work in my profession, in fashion. But the French wouldn't do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a friend of mine-- you'll see the next document-- told the French that I was working as his secretary. And that's how I be-- got a card to work, which is the next one that I will show you, which is a green card. I didn't have the right to work as a fashion designer, because they, they had enough fashion designers without having strangers from other countries doing that profession. So in general, this document allowed you to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the one that I'm going to show you-- this is just the right to stay in Paris. And every three months, you had to go and see the police and renew it. You had to constantly renew it. You couldn't get one just for a year or for two years. You had to renew it every three months. And they were all hoping that I would leave, which I did finally, going to America. Yeah, this is the card I had to be able to work in Paris officially. And I was-- it said that I was a secretary and interpreter because I spoke languages. For that, you could have a working card. But as a designer, you could not.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a friend of mine who had a business said that I worked for him. And this way, I could work for a fashion house. I could work altogether. Otherwise, I couldn't have. Did this replace the-- the previous document? Or did you still have to have that renewed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I still had to have the other document constantly renewed. You always had to in France, every three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the document the French gave me to travel to America, where I finally had my first visa to go to a country, which was extraordinary. It's called titre d'identite et de voyage. As I was nor French, nor Polish, nor Austrian, it just gave me a chance to travel with that, explaining who I was and my dates. And it was an incredible feeling to have a real visa and go to a country that accepted you. And this is a feeling I kept through my whole stay in America, a wonderful feeling, to be able to really belong somewhere. And the thing is, you come to America. You get a social security number. And you can work. You can work. It's open. You don't have to hide. You don't have to say that you are somebody else or that you do something else. You can work in your profession, which gave me my chance. And it was just extraordinary. I'm, I'm very thankful for that all my life. This is actually where I started my family, et cetera, et cetera, and where I still am. I love it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this issued?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was issued when I left France in June, 1951, to marry my husband in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were all of the documents issued out of Paris? Or did they have other offices?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, some were in Lyon-- no, no, it was wherever I stayed. No, only this and the two previous ones were issued in Paris. Because I only lived there for three years after finishing school in Switzerland. That's all, but three very important years for my life. Oh, these are my daughters, the best thing I ever did. One is Vivette on the left and Claudia on the right. Claudia is the older one. They are absolutely wonderful girls. They each are married, have their families. Vivette has a five-year-old little boy and live right-- lives right next to me, which is fantastic. Claudia used to do that, too. But she now lives in the Caribbean. She has two children. She has a little boy age 10, Kitt, and a little girl who is going to be two years old, whose name is Katalina. Kitt is named after an island, Saint Kitts, because she met her husband there. And Katalina is named after an island, too, because Kitt insisted that his sister also has to be an island. So that's the names. [LAUGHS] But Claudia is actually doing children's books. She does marvelous collages. And she had two children's books published by the Doubleday. And Vivette is a photo editor at magazine. You see, their professions are very much like ours. They've always-- I, I was always working during my whole life. They had somebody who took care of them, somebody very nice. But they saw me leave in the morning and come back and do something that I really loved. And I think it has formed them. They both wanted to do something interesting themselves, and they are doing it, which I'm very pleased about.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were they born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vivette-- Claudia, the older one, was born in 1956. Vivette was born in 1959. This is Kitt, my first grandson, and my mother's first great-grandson. And you can see how pleased she is to hold him. It's so wonderful that she could still be alive and have a grandchild. This was taken about 10 years ago. My mother was then nearly 90. And she was still living by herself, doing her own cooking, preparing for the holidays, which we always spend at her house. It was really extraordinary the-- how do you say that-- how lively she was, her vitality. Because my father died in 1962, and she just went on with her life-- went to opera, used to travel to Europe to see her family. And she was really an absolutely marvelous woman. Until three months before her death, she was totally self-sufficient-- great.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jetty, Jetty Eisenstab.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the little baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's little Kit, Kitt Porges-Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is [? 'PPP' ?] and I, taken in our little country house. It's actually-- it was a little cottage with a porch. And 20 years ago, we converted it to a little all-white house, which we adore. It's at a lake. We have a tree in the deck and lots of flower pots. And it's a wonderful, quiet place to relax after both of our professions. And I call it my smiling house. I mean, the minute we get there, it, it's just wonderful. It's a total smile on everybody's face. I love it. And America made this possible. It's great living here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: That must have been about maybe four years ago, which is around 1990, 1991, I would say.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is the picture that was taken this year when I found my grandparents' grave in Vienna. I just couldn't believe it. It was extraordinary. It-- it's just such a wonderful thing to have found something that pertained to them. I didn't know it all these years. I just found out at the Jewish community. And then I, I went out. A friend of mine came with me to the cemetery. They were not sure that we could find the spot. They just had a number. And we looked and looked. And then finally-- and I was going to have a stone put with their names-- I wanted to do all that. And then finally, my friend said, look, I think-- come on the other side-- I think this might be it. And it was. I just couldn't believe it. I think my uncle did that right after the war. But we never talked about it. Nobody said anything. I had to find this out after so many years by myself. But I'm glad I did. I think it's, it's, it's just wonderful. At least there's something to remember them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what does the stone say?

speaker: interviewee
text: It just says their names, when my grandfather was born, when he died-- he was 70 years old. And it says the name of my grandmother, but the an-- and it they just-- it just says that she died in Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are the names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's Salamon Weichsel and Shewa Weichsel. OK. I took the map because it was very hard to find. It's part of the Viennese Jewish Cemetery. But the people that are buried there, it's like a potter's field. It's just nothing-- it's not elaborate at all. There's just grass. It's not very well taken care of. And lots of graves are totally unmarked. There are no stones. So through this map, we found it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where on the map is the stone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it's about where it says number 21, 22, around there somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: The upper left hand?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that area predominantly Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and it's predominantly people that their ashes were sent back from concentration camps. And they were only sent back right in the beginning. They didn't do that anymore. It just became too many.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the cemetery in general, is it Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: The cemetery, no-- it's a tremendous cemetery, only it has four doors, four parts. And the Jewish cemetery is the fourth door. Now there are lots of graves there from before, elaborate graves, all kinds. But this is a very desolate, small area.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where in the city is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a little bit out of the city. It's, I think, in the 12th District. You take a streetcar. And you go out for like-- I don't know-- 20 minutes. That's where it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were the pictures taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken this year in June. I was in Vienna in 1995 in June. And that's when I went to the Jewish organization and wanted really to find out if they know anything about it. And it's the first time I, I heard some details about my grand-- grandparents' death. My parents didn't talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is it being maintained?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it is being maintained. And I'll see to it that it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Under whose authority?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, under the Jewish community in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I'd like to thank you for being a part of this, creating this document that will certainly be valuable for, for years to come and an important, an important step in documenting this, this, this period of time. And we appreciate your-- your involvement.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm very happy that I could do it. Thank you.
